explicit_text: I was born in Roccamarice, a small town in the Pescara province of Italy, on July 23, 1925. It was a peaceful place where I had a wonderful childhood with my family. However, life became difficult there when the Fascist government took control, and my family decided to leave for a better future.

explicit_text: Pescara is the province in Italy where my hometown, Roccamarice, is located. Growing up there in the 1920s and 30s, I experienced the challenges of living under Mussolini's regime. It was from this region that my family decided to emigrate to the United States seeking better opportunities.

explicit_text: My family and I immigrated to the United States when I was six years old, after being admitted through Ellis Island in the early 1930s. Growing up in America, specifically in Portland, Maine, I learned English and adapted to a new culture. Later, I moved to Chicago to pursue my studies and build a life, marrying William Fray and raising my family here.

explicit_text: After arriving in the United States, my family settled in Portland, Maine, where I grew up. It was there that I attended Portland High School and learned English, despite the challenges of being an Italian immigrant in the 1930s and 40s. My traditional Italian family lived there while I later moved to Chicago for further education.

explicit_text: In 1942, I moved to Chicago to study at the Art Institute of Chicago, which was an exciting new chapter in my life. It was also in Chicago that I met and married William Fray in the early 1950s, and eventually raised my family in the suburbs.

explicit_text: The rise of Benito Mussolini and his Fascist government in Italy made life very difficult for my family in Roccamarice. They even took over a ski resort owned by part of my family, which was a turning point that pushed us to leave Italy. Mussolini's regime was a scary time, and seeking a better life in the United States became our hope.

explicit_text: My mother, sister, and I first tried to enter the United States through Ellis Island when I was five, but we were denied because of an eye problem I had. After a year of treatment back in Italy, we returned and were finally admitted through Ellis Island when I was six. This marked the beginning of our new life in America.

My two brothers served in the U.S. Army during the war, which was a source of pride and concern for our family. The war years shaped much of my young adult experience in America.

explicit_text: Rome, or Roma as I call it, has always held a special place in my heart as the Eternal City of Italy. I often wished for my grandchildren to bring me back there one day to reconnect with my roots. Despite living most of my life in the United States, my love for Italy and Rome remained strong.

explicit_text: I have always loved opera, which is a beautiful part of Italian culture that I cherished throughout my life. It connected me to my heritage even while living far from Italy in the United States. Opera was one of the many ways I kept my Italian spirit alive in my family and community.

explicit_text: Later in life, I lived in Geneva, Illinois, a small western suburb of Chicago, where I spent the last sixty years of my life. It was there that I raised my family with William and embraced the blend of cultures we represented. Though far from Italy, Geneva became my home and the place where my family grew.
